narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know how disappointing it must feel to receive a rejection based on our predictive model's assessment of your bar exam prospects. The model looked at your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, family income bracket, and whether you'd be studying full-time, to forecast your chances of passing the bar on the first try. It estimated a 40.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which unfortunately crossed our threshold for denying admission to protect both you and the school's outcomes. The primary reason for this prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tier of your school that year—far below the typical student's mid-range standing—and this stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a failure. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, as it actually nudged the prediction toward success compared to students from higher-income backgrounds. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the average for applicants, and your full-time study plans helped pull the odds toward passing, much like they do for most students. Your Year 1 decile in the lower third and LSAT score of 32, both below typical mid-to-high averages, played smaller supporting roles but didn't outweigh the Year 3 concern. Overall, while many admitted students have stronger late-stage academic deciles, your profile shows real strengths in GPA and dedication to full-time study that could shine with improvement. To move forward, I encourage you to focus on boosting your academic performance in advanced coursework, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups tailored for Black women in law prep, and retake the LSAT to aim closer to the 36-37 average. Consider programs offering bar passage support for underrepresented students, and reapply after addressing that key Year 3 gap—many have turned similar predictions around. You've got resilience on your side; this is just one data point, not your destiny. Keep pushing, and we're rooting for you.
